title: Fall of the Berlin Wall
summary: On the evening of 9 November 1989 an East German politburo spokesman, Günter Schabowski, told a televised press conference that new travel regulations would take effect, as far as he knew, immediately. Within hours crowds were pressing at the checkpoints of the Berlin Wall. At 23:30 the officer in charge at Bornholmer Strasse, Harald Jäger, ordered his gates opened, and a barrier that had divided the city for twenty-eight years was simply walked over. The German Democratic Republic did not survive the year; the Soviet bloc did not survive the next two.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Berlin Wall was the concrete residue of the geography of defeat in 1945. When Germany surrendered in May of that year, the Allied powers divided the country and its capital into four occupation zones. As the wartime alliance dissolved between 1945 and 1949 the three western zones fused into the Federal Republic of Germany with its capital at Bonn, and the Soviet zone became the German Democratic Republic (GDR), with East Berlin as its capital. West Berlin, an enclave of the Federal Republic surrounded by GDR territory, remained a jurisdictional oddity, a legal foothold of the western Allies inside the Warsaw Pact. Between 1949 and 1961 some three and a half million East Germans, disproportionately young and skilled, migrated to the West across what was still an easily crossable inner-city border.

On the night of 12 to 13 August 1961, on orders from GDR leader Walter Ulbricht and with Soviet approval, East German soldiers and border guards strung barbed wire along the perimeter of West Berlin and closed the sector boundary. Within weeks concrete replaced wire. Over the next quarter-century the barrier hardened into an elaborate system: two parallel walls up to four metres high, a raked sand strip, anti-vehicle ditches, dog runs, floodlights, and around three hundred watchtowers along its 155-kilometre length. At least 140 people died attempting to cross it, from the eighteen-year-old bricklayer Peter Fechter, who bled to death in the death strip in August 1962 within sight of West Berlin, to Chris Gueffroy, the last person shot at the Wall in February 1989. The Wall became the totemic image of the Cold War, given voice in speeches by John F. Kennedy at Rudolph Wilde Platz in June 1963 (Ich bin ein Berliner) and Ronald Reagan at the Brandenburg Gate in June 1987 (Mr Gorbachev, tear down this wall).

By the summer of 1989 the Warsaw Pact was in visible dissolution. Mikhail Gorbachev, general secretary of the Soviet Communist Party since March 1985, had used his policies of perestroika and glasnost to loosen central controls; more consequentially, he had privately signalled to the leaders of the satellite states that Soviet troops would no longer intervene to prop up communist regimes as they had in East Germany in 1953, Hungary in 1956, or Czechoslovakia in 1968. This so-called Sinatra doctrine, that each country could go its own way, was tested first in Poland, where partial elections on 4 June 1989 produced a landslide for the Solidarity trade union and led to the appointment of Tadeusz Mazowiecki, a Catholic intellectual, as the first non-communist prime minister in the bloc since 1948. Hungary followed. On 2 May the Hungarian government began cutting down the barbed wire along its Austrian frontier; on 19 August, at the Pan-European Picnic at Sopron, hundreds of East German tourists in Hungary walked across into Austria unimpeded. Between then and the autumn tens of thousands of East Germans fled to the West via Hungary, then via Prague, where they crowded the grounds of the West German embassy.

Inside the GDR, protests mounted through the autumn. Weekly Monday demonstrations at the Nikolaikirche in Leipzig, initially in the low thousands, swelled to seventy thousand on 9 October, three hundred thousand a week later, and half a million on the last Monday of October. On 18 October the aged and cancer-stricken party leader Erich Honecker, in power since 1971, was forced from office by his Politburo colleagues and replaced by Egon Krenz, who promised reform without knowing quite what he meant. The exodus continued: by early November more than a hundred thousand East Germans had fled abroad, others were leaving daily via Czechoslovakia after the border was reopened on 1 November, and factories were losing skilled workers overnight. On 4 November a demonstration on Alexanderplatz in the centre of East Berlin drew perhaps half a million people. The Krenz Politburo, meeting in an atmosphere of near-panic, tried to reassert some initiative by drafting new travel regulations that would in principle allow East Germans to apply for private visits or permanent emigration to the West through any border crossing.

image.caption: Section of the Berlin Wall during its operational years, showing the concrete slabs, watchtower and cleared death strip.
image.credit: Imperial War Museums HU 99520, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The draft was rushed. Ministers of the interior and of state security had prepared it that morning of 9 November, intending that visa offices would begin issuing the new permits from the following day, after formal announcement through the official news agency. The politburo member designated to present the news to the international press was Günter Schabowski, a former newspaper editor now serving as party spokesman. He had been at a Central Committee meeting all afternoon, had not been briefed on the details of the travel decree, and received the text from Krenz in a brown paper folder as he left for the international press centre at Mohrenstrasse.

Schabowski's press conference opened at 18:00 and proceeded through routine business for nearly an hour, broadcast live on East German television. At 18:53 the Italian ANSA correspondent Riccardo Ehrman, sitting in the front row, asked when the new travel law would come into force. Schabowski leafed through his papers, read a summary of the decree aloud haltingly, and, asked again for clarification, replied: as far as I know, immediately, without delay (Das tritt nach meiner Kenntnis ist das sofort, unverzüglich). Pressed on whether the regulation applied to West Berlin, he said yes. Neither he nor his questioners quite understood what he had just done. West German television, which nearly every East German household watched, broadcast the news on the ZDF evening bulletin at 19:17: The gates of the wall stand wide open, said the anchor Hans Joachim Friedrichs on the flagship ARD Tagesthemen at 22:42, in an announcement that has entered the German national archive.

At East Berlin's border crossings, guards had received no operational orders. Small crowds began to gather from about 20:00, growing rapidly. At Bornholmer Strasse in Prenzlauer Berg, which crossed the S-Bahn tracks on the Bösebrücke bridge into the French sector of West Berlin, the officer of the passport control unit was Lieutenant-Colonel Harald Jäger, a career Stasi officer. Jäger and his forty-five men faced by 22:00 an estimated twenty thousand East Berliners chanting Tor auf, Tor auf, open the gate, open the gate. Jäger telephoned his superiors repeatedly for orders and received only equivocation. Around 22:30 he began letting through, in ones and twos, the loudest and most insistent, invalidating their identity cards so they could not return, an improvised policy he later called a valve. The pressure only grew. At 23:30, on his own responsibility, Jäger ordered the barrier raised and control abandoned. Tens of thousands of East Berliners then flooded across the Bösebrücke, and the other crossings, Chausseestrasse, Invalidenstrasse, Sonnenallee, Checkpoint Charlie in Friedrichstrasse, opened in cascade over the following hour.

Across Berlin that night the scenes were extraordinary. West Berliners handed champagne to arriving Easterners, or lifted them onto the concrete slabs of the Wall in front of the Brandenburg Gate; strangers embraced in the streets; Trabant cars queued along the Kurfürstendamm. Chunks of the Wall were chipped off by souvenir-hunters who quickly acquired the name Mauerspechte, wall-woodpeckers. The Berlin Philharmonic Orchestra, home from a tour, gathered in the Philharmonie for an impromptu open-doors concert; the Staatsoper on Unter den Linden did the same. On 10 November the Federal Republic offered each East German visitor a symbolic hundred-mark welcome payment (Begrüssungsgeld). Chancellor Helmut Kohl, on a state visit to Warsaw, cut his trip short and flew to Berlin, where he addressed a rally in front of the Schöneberg town hall on the evening of 10 November alongside the elderly former chancellor Willy Brandt, who had been governing mayor of West Berlin when the Wall went up in 1961 and who now spoke the memorable line: now what belongs together grows together (Jetzt wächst zusammen, was zusammen gehört).

image.caption: West and East Berliners on top of the Berlin Wall in front of the Brandenburg Gate, days after 9 November 1989.
image.credit: Public documentation at Brandenburg Gate, reproduced by Lear 21, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The opening of the Wall did not by itself unite Germany, but it removed the physical obstacle to unity and made the political question unavoidable. The Krenz government tried to convert the movement into a controlled reform of the GDR under a renamed communist party, and appointed the reformist Hans Modrow as prime minister at the end of November. Kohl responded on 28 November with a ten-point plan for a confederation between the two Germanies, without prior consultation of his western allies. Free elections in the GDR on 18 March 1990 produced a decisive victory for the East German Christian Democrats aligned with Kohl on a platform of rapid unification. The Deutsche Mark was extended to the GDR on 1 July 1990. Formal reunification followed on 3 October 1990 under Article 23 of the Federal Republic's Basic Law, absorbing the five re-established eastern Länder into the Federal Republic. On 20 June 1991 the Bundestag voted narrowly to move the seat of government from Bonn back to Berlin, which had not been the German capital since 1945.

The international framework was negotiated with more delicacy. The four wartime Allies still had residual occupation rights over Germany as a whole. Kohl's foreign minister Hans-Dietrich Genscher and the American secretary of state James Baker devised the Two Plus Four talks, in which the two German governments negotiated with the four Allied powers. The Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany, signed in Moscow on 12 September 1990, ended those rights, confirmed Germany's Oder-Neisse frontier with Poland, and limited the size of the united German army. British prime minister Margaret Thatcher and French president François Mitterrand had privately opposed rapid unification, fearing a resurgent German dominance; Mitterrand secured his acquiescence in return for German agreement to accelerate the European monetary union that became the euro after the Maastricht Treaty of 1992.

The Wall itself was demolished piece by piece over the following months. Physical dismantling began officially on 13 June 1990 at Bernauer Strasse, and by November 1991 almost the entire wall had been broken up into some 1.7 million tons of concrete rubble, much of it recycled as motorway hardcore. Surviving fragments went to museums and to political leaders around the world; sections stand today at the Ronald Reagan Presidential Library, at the United Nations garden in New York, at the Vatican, and along parts of the original line in Berlin such as the East Side Gallery, where more than a hundred artists painted the eastern face of a 1.3 kilometre surviving stretch in 1990.

The fall of the Wall accelerated the collapse of the Warsaw Pact and of the Soviet Union itself. The Velvet Revolution in Czechoslovakia began ten days later on 17 November 1989; the Bulgarian regime fell on 10 November; the Romanian Ceausescu regime was overthrown at Christmas after a bloody rising in Timisoara. The Warsaw Pact was formally dissolved in July 1991; the Soviet Union followed on 26 December 1991. The Cold War, whose defining icon the Berlin Wall had been, was over.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
For Germans the ninth of November occupies a paradoxical place in the national calendar, as the day of both the 1989 opening of the Wall and the 1938 Kristallnacht pogrom against Jewish businesses and synagogues. That coincidence prevented the date from becoming the anniversary of German unification, an honour that instead went to 3 October, the day of the formal treaty in 1990. But 9 November remains the emotional anniversary, marked in Berlin each year by candlelit ceremonies along the former line of the Wall at Bernauer Strasse and the Berlin Wall Memorial there, opened in stages between 1998 and 2014.

The political and economic integration of the two halves of Germany took much longer than most expected in 1989. The transfer of some two trillion Deutschmarks in reconstruction funds through the Solidarity surcharge (Solidaritätszuschlag) into the east could not fully close the productivity gap. Eastern voting patterns produced first the successor Party of Democratic Socialism, then Die Linke, and later the far-right Alternative für Deutschland, giving eastern politics a distinct texture that persists. Unemployment in the east ran at twice western levels for a decade, out-migration of the young hollowed out towns from Rostock to Görlitz, and social scientists spoke of an ossi/wessi divide of mentality that has been slow to close.

For the wider world the fall of the Wall became the founding image of a triumphalist post-Cold War era. The American political scientist Francis Fukuyama, writing in the summer of 1989, argued that history in the Hegelian sense had ended with the victory of liberal democracy; his essay The End of History became one of the defining texts of the 1990s. That confidence, in retrospect, did not survive the following three decades: NATO's eastward expansion, the wars in the Balkans, the return of authoritarian politics in Russia under Vladimir Putin, and the twenty-first-century arguments over democratic backsliding across eastern Europe have all complicated the story. But the images from Berlin on the night of 9 November 1989, of ordinary people climbing the Wall in front of the Brandenburg Gate with sledgehammers and champagne, remain among the most durable moving pictures of the twentieth century, and, for many who watched them at the time or since, one of its few unambiguously happy endings.

worldContext.text: China in 1989 experienced a very different revolutionary spring. From April, following the death of the reformist party leader Hu Yaobang, students occupied Tiananmen Square in Beijing demanding political liberalisation; by mid-May a million people were on the square and the movement had spread to over a hundred Chinese cities. On the night of 3 to 4 June the People's Liberation Army entered Beijing under martial law and cleared the square with tanks and live ammunition, killing many hundreds (estimates range from around three hundred to over a thousand). Deng Xiaoping's decision to crush the movement marked a durable divergence from the eastern European trajectory: China preserved single-party rule while pursuing continuing economic liberalisation. In Japan, the Shōwa emperor Hirohito died on 7 January 1989, opening the Heisei era under his son Akihito.

The Middle East in 1989 was in transition. Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, founder of the Islamic Republic of Iran, died on 3 June aged eighty-six; Ali Khamenei was named Supreme Leader the next day, and the pragmatic Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani was elected president in July, opening a period of postwar reconstruction after the eight-year war with Iraq that had ended in August 1988. In February Khomeini had issued a fatwa against the British novelist Salman Rushdie for The Satanic Verses. Saddam Hussein's Iraq began the drawn-out disputes with Kuwait that would culminate in the invasion of August 1990. In Lebanon the Taif Agreement in October formally ended the fifteen-year civil war.

In South Africa on 15 August 1989 President P. W. Botha resigned after a stroke and internal party revolt, and was succeeded by F. W. de Klerk. Within weeks de Klerk began meeting Nelson Mandela in prison; on 15 October, he released Walter Sisulu and seven other veteran African National Congress leaders. Public marches were tolerated for the first time in decades. In February 1990 de Klerk would unban the ANC and release Mandela after twenty-seven years' imprisonment, opening the negotiations that led to the end of apartheid. Angola's civil war, kept alive by Cuban and South African intervention, wound down after Cuban troops withdrew under the terms of the 1988 Tripartite Accord. Namibia held its first free election in November 1989 en route to independence in March 1990.

The United States in 1989 was in the first year of George H. W. Bush's presidency after his inauguration on 20 January; Bush spent much of the year following the eastern European upheavals with cautious care not to embarrass Gorbachev. At the Malta summit of 2 to 3 December he and Gorbachev declared the Cold War at an end. On 20 December Bush ordered the invasion of Panama to capture the strongman Manuel Noriega on drug-trafficking charges; Noriega surrendered in early January. In Chile Augusto Pinochet, defeated in the plebiscite of October 1988, allowed presidential elections on 14 December 1989 that were won by the Christian Democrat Patricio Aylwin, ending sixteen years of military dictatorship. Brazil elected its first directly chosen president since 1960.

In February 1989 the last Soviet troops crossed the Friendship Bridge out of Afghanistan under the command of General Boris Gromov, ending a nine-year occupation that had cost some fifteen thousand Soviet lives and vastly more Afghan ones. The Soviet-backed government of Mohammad Najibullah in Kabul now had to fight on alone against the US and Pakistan-armed mujahideen; it would fall in April 1992. India in 1989 held a general election in late November that produced a hung parliament and brought down the government of Rajiv Gandhi; V. P. Singh formed a minority coalition. Pakistan under Benazir Bhutto, the first female head of an Islamic state's government, was in her first term as prime minister, elected the previous December.

The Australian government of Labor prime minister Bob Hawke, in office since 1983, was in 1989 pushing through the deregulatory economic reforms designed with Treasurer Paul Keating that had already floated the Australian dollar and dismantled tariff protection. In New Zealand the fourth Labour government under Geoffrey Palmer continued the Rogernomics restructuring begun by David Lange, and the country was in the middle of a period of anti-nuclear diplomacy that had suspended its ANZUS treaty relations with the United States. In the smaller Pacific island states, decolonisation was still incomplete: Palau would not achieve full independence until 1994, and French New Caledonia was recovering from the near-civil-war of the mid-1980s under the terms of the 1988 Matignon Accords.

figures.name: Günter Schabowski
figures.role: East German politburo spokesman whose 9 November press conference announcement, as far as I know, immediately, without delay, unintentionally triggered the opening of the Wall

figures.name: Harald Jäger
figures.role: Lieutenant-colonel of the Stasi passport control unit at the Bornholmer Strasse crossing, ordered his gates raised at 23:30 on his own responsibility after failing to obtain orders

figures.name: Egon Krenz
figures.role: General Secretary of the SED and head of state of the GDR since 18 October 1989, presided over the collapse of communist East Germany and resigned in December

figures.name: Helmut Kohl
figures.role: Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany, drove the negotiations for German reunification and served as first chancellor of the reunified country
figures.credit: European Communities, photo Christian Lambiotte (1996), CC BY 4.0

figures.name: Mikhail Gorbachev
figures.role: General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union, whose refusal to intervene militarily to prop up the eastern European regimes made 1989 possible
figures.credit: Vladimir Vyatkin / RIA Novosti, CC BY-SA 3.0

images.caption: West and East Berliners on top of the Berlin Wall in front of the Brandenburg Gate in the days after 9 November 1989, one of the defining images of the end of the Cold War.
images.credit: Photograph from a public documentation wall at the Brandenburg Gate, reproduced by Lear 21, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Section of the Berlin Wall during its operational years, showing the concrete slabs, guard tower, and cleared death strip that separated the two halves of the city between 1961 and 1989.
images.credit: Imperial War Museums, collection HU 99520, via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: The Berlin Wall Falls and USSR Dissolves
sources.publisher: US Department of State, Office of the Historian
